title: Internal Authenticated Vulnerability Scan Coverage by CDE Subnet
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the PCI DSS programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with PCI DSS control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 11.3.1.3 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 11.3.1.3 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.74: Internal Authenticated Vulnerability Scan Coverage by CDE Subnet.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.74' running on index=vm tenable:sc:vuln with scan_name, host_ip, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Internal Authenticated Vulnerability Scan Coverage by CDE Su' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper PCI DSS control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the PCI DSS control objective.

dataSources: `index=vm` `tenable:sc:vuln` with `scan_name`, `host_ip` — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=vm sourcetype="tenable:sc:vuln" earliest=-90d
| where match(scan_name,"(?i)internal|credentialed|CDE)")
| eval subnet=replace(host_ip,"^(\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+)\\.\\d+$","\\1.0/24")
| lookup pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv subnet OUTPUT required_scan
| where required_scan="true"
| stats latest(_time) as last_scan by subnet
| eval days_since=round((now()-last_scan)/86400,1)
| where days_since>90 OR isnull(last_scan)
| sort days_since
description: Verifies each CDE /24 received at least one credentialed scan in the rolling quarter so internal vulnerability scanning frequency and scope requirements are met with machine evidence.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PCI DSS compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes vm and sourcetypes tenable:sc:vuln. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Normalize IP field names from Tenable TA. (2) Adjust `subnet` extraction for IPv6 if in scope. (3) Alert subnets over 90 days. (4) Map exceptions to documented outage windows. Indexes required: `index=vm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=tenable:sc:vuln`; Lookups: `pci_cde_subnets_summary`, `pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv`.
visualization: Table (subnet, days_since), choropleth if subnets mapped to sites, gauge (worst gap).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t dc(Vulnerabilities.dest) as agg_value from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.severity, Vulnerabilities.signature, Vulnerabilities.dest | sort - agg_value
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Vulnerabilities (CIM data model).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised penetration testing** — Annual or more frequent penetration tests conducted by QSA-approved assessors against cardholder data environment (CDE) systems produce expected security alerts that are pre-communicated and tracked via the test engagement letter.

2. **PCI ASV scanning activity** — Quarterly external vulnerability scans performed by Approved Scanning Vendors generate network probe events that trigger IDS/IPS alerts as an expected part of the PCI DSS validation process.

3. **Tokenization system batch processing** — The payment tokenization platform performs scheduled batch re-tokenization and token lifecycle management operations that produce high-volume cardholder data access patterns within approved operational windows.

4. **Acquirer and processor settlement activity** — End-of-day settlement reconciliation between the merchant platform and payment processor involves legitimate PAN/SAD data transmission that is covered by the processor service provider agreement.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `pci_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved pen-test engagement IDs, ASV scan source IPs, scheduled batch job names, and settlement processing windows. The search filters matches where `pci_exception_valid=true` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Tenable Add-on for Splunk" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `vm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `11.3.1.3`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI DSS 11.3.1.3 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.74: Internal Authenticated Vulnerability Scan Coverage by CDE Subnet." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060), landing events in `vm` under `tenable:sc:vuln`. Daily vulnerability deltas for a 10000-asset estate produce 50000-500000 findings/day (~5-50 GB/month). Full snapshots weekly add more.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > Tenable.sc Vulnerability. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[tenable_sc_vuln://prod]
endpoint = https://tenable.example.com
interval = 86400
index = vulnerability
sourcetype = tenable:sc:vuln
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `pluginID, severity, state, first_seen, last_seen, host, dns, ip, plugin_name, cve`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=vm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Normalize IP field names from Tenable TA. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

• **Hint 2:** Adjust `subnet` extraction for IPv6 if in scope. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Alert subnets over 90 days. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Map exceptions to documented outage windows. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Internal Authenticated Vulnerability Scan Coverage by CDE Subnet," the aggregation measures control coverage — the output rows enumerate what IS covered, and an auditor's follow-on question ('what about the rest?') is answered by the gap between this result and the authoritative population.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv` — the authoritative Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) scope register. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`required_scan`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `subnet`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval subnet = lower(subnet)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 90 days.

Numeric threshold `days_since > 90`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Vulnerabilities` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t dc(Vulnerabilities.dest) as agg_value from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.severity, Vulnerabilities.signature, Vulnerabilities.dest | sort - agg_value
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Vulnerabilities` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.74: Internal Authenticated Vulnerability Scan Coverage by CDE Su]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.74,reg=PCI_DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=vm sourcetype=tenable:sc:vuln earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `days_since`, `last_scan`, `subnet`. Run:

```spl
index=vm sourcetype=tenable:sc:vuln earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table days_since, last_scan, subnet
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup pci_cde_subnets_summary.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_since`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_since) as p50 perc90(days_since) as p90 perc99(days_since) as p99 max(days_since) as hi
```

The current threshold `90` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.74: PCI DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`subnet`, `days_since`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Choropleth If Subnets Mapped To Sites (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Gauge (`worst gap`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We verifies each CDE /24 received at least one credentialed scan in the rolling quarter so internal vulnerability scanning frequency and scope requirements are met with machine evidence. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
